title: URL Allowlisting, Untrusted API Data, Terminal Safety, Files, and Resource Abuse · Contract Triage
summary: A preserved Go Pokedex run contains one plausible command, parsing, URL, JSON, client, cache, ownership, goroutine, cancellation, persistence, security, test, packaging, or release defect; locate the first failed invariant before editing. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in deny-by-default Pokedex trust-boundary policy and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, a…
objectives: Revalidate scheme, host, port, redirect count, and destination policy on every hop and reject user-controlled egress.
Normalize or visibly escape control, bidi, and ANSI sequences from API, file, and user data before terminal presentation.
Choose application-owned storage roots, reject traversal or unexpected file types, and minimize permission and symlink assumptions.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.text: A debug Go team handles case 1364 during a narrow-terminal ranger workflow. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce destination, terminal, file, privacy, and resource trust boundaries; failed and canceled commands must preserve the prior admitted state; then change one command, argument, EOF, or output-width condition and preserve the session contract. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original fixtures only. Real PokéAPI traffic, DNS, sockets, TLS, host files, terminals, signals, race and leak claims, load, cross-platform packaging, accessibility review, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Bound command length, response bytes, decoded records, cache, history, collection, concurrency, retries, output, and persistence size.

Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: pokedex-go-resource-abuse-defense-1htf96" that uses case 5757 to enforce destination, terminal, file, privacy, and resource trust boundaries. Keep defaults runnable and return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not open sockets, resolve DNS, contact PokéAPI, read or write host files, handle real terminal or OS signals, spawn unbounded work, or claim race, leak, load, accessibility, cross-platform, package, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5, original fixtures, controlled RoundTrippers and httptest servers, race and fuzz runs, temporary files, subprocess and signal harnesses, inspected artifacts, representative platforms, accessibility review, capacity, rollback, recovery, and release gates.
